notes.anchor: the leather screens of your earlier crossing
notes.note: Greek derreis: the leather weather-screens rigged on a ship's deck for rough passages; Cicero recalls Atticus's earlier stormy crossing with relief that this one was smooth.
A pun on Atticus's name: the plain, elegant 'Attic' style of oratory, here transferred jocularly to the banker Vestorius's company that detains Atticus in Campania.

notes.anchor: the cash openly distributed before the elections tribe by tribe
notes.note: Open, brazen bribery (divisio) organized by voting tribe; Cicero's bitter shorthand for the collapse of electoral order in 54 BC.
The persistent rumour that Pompey would be made dictator amid the electoral paralysis; an office dormant and feared since Sulla.

notes.anchor: the one plank from the shipwreck
notes.note: A favourite Ciceronian image: amid the wreck of the free state, his friendship with Caesar (and Caesar's favour to his brother Quintus) is the single spar he clings to.
Cicero accepted a nominal legateship (legatio libera) under Pompey, who as Spanish proconsul governed through deputies while remaining near Rome; it let Cicero keep a foot outside the city.
13 January; the Ides fell on the thirteenth in most months. Cicero notes he will be outside the city bounds from that date in his new role as Pompey's legate.
